claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with a few basic questions:

1. **Do you have any children?** If yes, how many children are you asking about, and what are their names and ages?

This will help me understand your situation and guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - Alex, who is 13 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, who is 13 years old.

Let me ask you some questions to assess your eligibility:

**Does Alex normally live with you?** (This means they live with you for the majority of the time, even if they occasionally stay elsewhere like with another parent or relative)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me - they live with their other parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask:

**Are you contributing to Alex's upbringing?** This could include:
- Paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent amount for Alex) towards their upkeep each week
- Providing financial support for their care, clothing, food, or other needs

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute to Alex's upbringing. I help with their needs - things like clothes, food, and other expenses they need.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To determine your eligibility, I need to understand the amount you're contributing.

**Are you paying at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit rate each week towards Alex's upbringing?** 

For context, the current Child Benefit rate for one child is approximately £25.60 per week (though rates can change). Are you contributing at least this amount or more per week towards Alex's costs (clothes, food, expenses, etc.)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute about that amount each week - it works out to around £26 per week that I spend on Alex's needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Now I need to ask about the other parent:

**Is the other parent (the one Alex lives with) claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment:

1. **Are you responsible for Alex?** This means you have parental responsibility or are otherwise responsible for them (e.g., as a parent, guardian, or someone who has taken on responsibility for their care).

2. **Do you live in the UK?**

Let me start with the first one - are you Alex's parent or do you have parental responsibility for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm Alex's parent, so I am responsible for them. But no, I don't live in the UK - I live abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. Unfortunately, this affects your eligibility significantly.

One of the key requirements for Child Benefit is that **you must live in the UK** (or in some specific circumstances, be subject to UK National Insurance contributions if you're a Crown servant or member of HM Forces posted overseas).

Let me ask: **Are you working abroad as a Crown servant (civil servant) or as a member of HM Forces?** These are specific exceptions where you might still be eligible even though you're living outside the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or a member of HM Forces. I'm just living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, unfortunately **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**. Here's why:

**The main issue is residency:** One of the fundamental requirements for Child Benefit is that you must be living in the UK. Since you're living abroad and you're not a Crown servant or member of HM Forces (which are specific exceptions to this rule), you do not meet this essential eligibility criterion.

While you do meet other requirements - you're Alex's parent, you're contributing financially to their upbringing at the required level, and the other parent isn't claiming - the UK residency requirement is mandatory and cannot be waived in your situation.

**What this means:** You cannot claim Child Benefit while living abroad unless you fall into one of the specific exempted categories (Crown servants, HM Forces, or certain other limited circumstances involving UK National Insurance contributions).

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?
